macro_events.event_summary: Senator Warren is pressing the Trump administration on evacuation of Americans amid an active Iran war, confirming ongoing US-Iran military conflict.
primary_drivers: Middle East oil supply risk premium (Strait of Hormuz exposure)
Elevated defense spending and munitions replenishment demand
Flight-to-safety bid in defensives and energy

beneficiaries.rationale: Lockheed Martin is the prime contractor for THAAD and PAC-3 missile interceptors actively used against Iranian ballistic missiles; an active war drives immediate munitions replenishment orders and supplemental defense appropriations.
Raytheon makes Patriot, SM-3/SM-6, Tomahawk, and AMRAAM — all consumed heavily in a Gulf conflict. Direct revenue uplift from DoD replenishment contracts typically signed within weeks of expenditure.
L3Harris provides ISR, tactical radios, and electronic warfare systems with direct CENTCOM exposure; active conflict accelerates procurement timelines.

ExxonMobil is the most liquid integrated oil major; any Strait of Hormuz disruption risk (20% of global seaborne oil) directly lifts Brent, widening upstream margins. Limited direct Iran/Gulf production exposure means pure price leverage.
ConocoPhillips is a pure-play upstream E&P with no refining drag and no Iranian/Gulf producing assets at risk — highest beta to a Brent spike among US majors.
Kratos supplies loitering munitions, target drones, and tactical UAVs increasingly used in CENTCOM operations; small base means any wartime order is materially accretive.
General Dynamics benefits via munitions (Ordnance & Tactical Systems) plus combat systems; also owns Gulfstream which sees evacuation/charter demand spike.
Teekay Tankers — product tanker rates spike on Hormuz rerouting and war-risk insurance premiums; direct beneficiary of tonne-mile expansion as cargoes avoid Gulf.

losers.rationale: Delta has significant transatlantic and codeshare exposure; higher jet fuel prices from Brent spike plus Middle East airspace closures force costly reroutes. Jet fuel is ~25% of operating costs.
United has the most international long-haul exposure of US carriers including India/South Asia routes that transit or skirt Iranian airspace, forcing fuel-burning reroutes on top of fuel cost spike.

Southwest is unhedged on fuel as of recent disclosures and runs thin margins; domestic-only but still exposed to full Brent-driven jet fuel passthrough with no international premium pricing to offset.
Carnival has cancelled/rerouted Middle East and Red Sea itineraries in prior regional conflicts; bunker fuel costs rise with Brent and consumer discretionary travel demand softens on war headlines.
Marriott has meaningful Middle East property exposure (UAE, Saudi, Qatar, Jordan) with evacuations and travel advisories directly hitting RevPAR in the region.
Trucking/LTL operators face diesel cost spike from crude jump; XPO's thin operating margins amplify fuel passthrough lag.

headlines: Warren presses Trump administration for answers on evacuation of Americans amid Iran war

macro_events.event_summary: Trump extended the Iran ceasefire, reducing Middle East geopolitical risk premium, while markets await Tesla earnings.
primary_drivers: Lower geopolitical risk premium in crude oil
Improved risk-on sentiment lowering shipping/insurance risk through Strait of Hormuz

beneficiaries.rationale: Jet fuel is ~20-25% of airline operating costs; a sustained ceasefire caps crude and directly improves margins. Delta has limited hedging vs peers so gets full benefit of spot declines.
Same jet fuel cost sensitivity as DAL; United also has meaningful international/transatlantic exposure where lower oil + reduced Mideast airspace risk both help route economics.
Cruise operator with ~10-12% of costs in bunker fuel; lower Brent from ceasefire extension directly compresses fuel expense and lifts margin estimates.
Earnings catalyst tonight — unrelated to ceasefire but explicitly flagged in headline as the binary event driving tape; options-implied move is the actual trade.

losers.rationale: Integrated major with large upstream earnings leverage to Brent; a ceasefire extension bleeds the geopolitical premium out of crude, pressuring E&P segment earnings estimates.
Pure-play US oil producer with high beta to WTI; loss of Iran-risk premium is a direct hit to realized price assumptions. Higher leverage amplifies the move vs XOM.
Defense prime with heavy missile/air-defense mix (PAC-3, THAAD, Javelin) that rallied on Iran tensions; ceasefire extension removes near-term urgency narrative for replenishment orders.

headlines: Dow Jones Futures Rise As Trump Extends Iran Ceasefire; Tesla Earnings Due
